item.title: Fiji Showcase 2026 is better with Smartworks by Narhari
item.description: THE countdown is over. Fiji Showcase 2026 is here, and Smartworks by Narhari is bringing together some of the most exciting ICT products and technology brands under one roof. From June 19 to 27 at Vodafone Arena, Suva, visit Booth G6 and discover the latest innovations designed to make work easier, entertainment better, and everyday life more connected. Whether you are upgrading your home office,
item.content:encoded:
THE countdown is over. Fiji Showcase 2026 is here, and Smartworks by Narhari is bringing together some of the most exciting ICT products and technology brands under one roof. From June 19 to 27 at Vodafone Arena, Suva, visit Booth G6 and discover the latest innovations designed to make work easier, entertainment better, and everyday life more connected.

Whether you are upgrading your home office, looking for a new smartphone, searching for the perfect tablet, or exploring the latest smart gadgets, this is your chance to see, touch, and experience the technology everyone will be talking about.

For productivity on the move, BMAX laptops deliver impressive performance, sleek designs, and excellent value. Perfect for students, professionals, and home users, BMAX combines power and portability to help you stay productive wherever life takes you.

For everyday communication and smart connectivity, Coolpad smartphones offer dependable performance, stylish designs, and practical features that keep you connected with family, friends, and work. They are designed for people who want great value without compromising on functionality.

For premium mini computing power, GEEKOM Mini PCs pack serious performance into a compact footprint. Ideal for offices, businesses, and home workspaces, they provide desktop-class productivity while saving valuable space.

For entertainment and versatility, MINIX solutions offer powerful mini-PCs and smart computing devices built for streaming, productivity, and digital lifestyles. Compact yet capable, they are designed to fit seamlessly into modern homes and offices.

For immersive sound and portable entertainment, TAZATA Bluetooth speakers bring music to life with powerful audio, stylish designs, and wireless convenience. Whether you are relaxing at home or enjoying time outdoors, TAZATA keeps the good vibes going.

For work, study, and entertainment, TECLAST tablets deliver large displays, responsive performance, and portability that make everyday tasks easier. Perfect for video calls, streaming, browsing, and learning on the go.

For charging, connectivity, and digital accessories, UGREEN offers trusted solutions that keep all your devices powered and connected. From chargers and cables to hubs and adapters, UGREEN helps simplify modern living.

For smart entertainment, AIWA combines stylish technology with impressive performance across televisions, monitors, and audio products. Designed for today’s connected homes, AIWA delivers exceptional value and reliability.

For mobile innovation, UMIDIGI smartphones provide modern features, long battery life, impressive cameras, and smooth everyday performance. Built for people who want more from their mobile experience, UMIDIGI offers technology that keeps pace with your lifestyle.

At Smartworks by Narhari, technology is more than just products. It is about helping people work smarter, stay connected, and enjoy life more. Visit Booth G6 at Fiji Showcase 2026 and take advantage of exclusive event specials, demonstrations, giveaways, and unbeatable deals available only during the showcase.

Visit Smartworks by Narhari at Booth G6.

Follow us for the latest deals, promos & tech updates on:

: https://www.tiktok.com/@narhari officialaccount

Visit our website: https://www.narharifiji.com/

From June 19 to 27 at the Vodafone Arena, Suva, visit the Fiji Showcase booth G6 and discover the latest innovations designed to make work easier, entertainment better, and everyday life more connected.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Enjoyed alone, or as an ingredient in your food
item.description: M&Ms are one of the world’s most iconic confectionary brands, instantly recognizable by their colourful candy shells and irresistible chocolate centers. A product of Mars Wrigley Confectionary (MWC), M&M’s have been delighting the world since the 1940s. Over the decades they have become a simple yet global phenomenon, available in countless flavours, varieties, and seasonal editions. Their popular
item.content:encoded:
M&Ms are one of the world’s most iconic confectionary brands, instantly recognizable by their colourful candy shells and irresistible chocolate centers. A product of Mars Wrigley Confectionary (MWC), M&M’s have been delighting the world since the 1940s.

Over the decades they have become a simple yet global phenomenon, available in countless flavours, varieties, and seasonal editions. Their popularity doesn’t just lie in its taste but also in their playful branding.

M&M’s holds a strong cultural presence. The iconic mascots which represent the seven flavours, all of which have their own unique personality, are created by Mars Wrigley Confectionary.

While M&M’s is enjoyed alone, it can also be used as an ingredient in your food. Their combination of crunch and chocolate makes it ideal for baking, and they can be easily incorporated into your baking creation.

½ tsp baking soda,1/8 tsp salt,1/3 cup softened unsalted butter, 1/3 cup granulated sugar,1 egg,

In a medium bowl, whisk together flour, cocoa powder, baking soda and salt

In a large bowl, beat together butter, brown sugar and granulated sugar until light and fluffy. Beat egg white and vanilla until smooth. Stir in flour mixture until just incorporated. Cover and chill dough for at least 30 minutes.

In a medium bowl, whisk together flour, baking soda & salt.

In a large bowl, using an electric mixer, beat together butter, brown sugar and granulated sugar until light and fluffy. Beat in egg yolk and vanilla until smooth.

Stir in flour mixture until just incorporated. Stir in half the M&M’s peanut minis. Cover and chill for at least 30 minutes.

Divide brownie cooking dough into 16 portions and roll into balls. Press together one brownie dough ball and one M&M’s cookie dough ball to form bigger dough ball. Transfer to baking paper and press to flatten.

Sprinkle remaining M&M’s Peanut Minis over the cookies.

Bake for 10-12 minutes or until lightly golden around the edges, and tops are set. Let it cool in the pan for 2 minutes, then transfer to wire rack to cool completely

A product of Mars Wrigley Confectionery (MWC), M&M’s is distributed in Fiji by Motibhai Group – sharing quality, sharing happiness.

Right: Tanushka Tanvi Prakash shops for M&M’s at New World, Damodar City, Centre, Suva.

In the 1980s, M&M’s officially became the first candy in space. Pictures:MOTIBHAI GROUP

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | VERE’S TAKE | Retailers should check for price tags
item.description: Bula vinaka shoppers, for many a trip to the supermarket has become an exercise in careful budgeting and thinking. Shoppers walk through aisles comparing prices, calculating what they can afford, and often putting items back on shelves as the cost of living continues to rise every week. In such difficult economic times, something as simple as a missing price tag is not a minor inconvenience anymor
item.content:encoded:
Bula vinaka shoppers, for many a trip to the supermarket has become an exercise in careful budgeting and thinking. Shoppers walk through aisles comparing prices, calculating what they can afford, and often putting items back on shelves as the cost of living continues to rise every week.

In such difficult economic times, something as simple as a missing price tag is not a minor inconvenience anymore, it is a failure of transparency and a disregard for the consumers who keep businesses operating.

Recent market surveillance by the Consumer Council of Fiji revealsthis worrying trend where so many products have missing displayed prices as well as shelf prices that do not match what is charged at the checkout.

This is not a new issue. But the fact that such findings continue to emerge despite repeated warnings raises serious questions on whether some supermarkets are doing enough to uphold their obligations to customers.

Supermarkets often explain these discrepancies by pointing to frequent price adjustments, staff shortages, human error, or technical failures in pricing systems. As a shopper what do you do when price tags are missing? Often, you would reach out to find a staff in the store to help.

While these challenges of missing price tags may be genuine, they cannot become an excuse for retailers.

Retailing is a business built on trust, and ensuring accurate pricing is one of the most basic responsibilities a business owes its customers.

The economic climate makes this issue even more significant.

Fiji, like many countries, has experienced increased pressure from global supply chain disruptions, rising shipping costs, inflation, and fluctuating import prices.

These factors should force supermarkets to adjust prices more regularly, and also increase the need for stronger monitoring systems, better staff training, and more frequent shelf audits. For the average consumer, the impact of a missing or incorrect price tag goes beyond inconvenience.

It creates uncertainty at a time when every dollar matters.

A parent shopping for a family may choose one product over another because it appears more affordable, only to discover at the checkout that the actual price is higher. Others may avoid buying an item altogether because there is no price displayed and they do not wish to face embarrassment or confusion at the cashier. That is not fair.

Transparent pricing is a fundamental consumer right.

Shoppers should not have to search for a staff to ask the cost of basic goods, nor should they have to constantly question whether the price displayed on a shelf is the same price that will appear on their receipt.

The responsibility to provide clear and accurate pricing lies with the retailer, not the customer. The persistence of these breaches also highlights the need for stronger accountability. Supermarkets should not wait for inspections by regulators before correcting problems. Daily checks improved digital inventory systems, dedicated staff responsible for price verification, and a culture of compliance should become standard practice. At the same time, consumers must be encouraged to speak up.

Reporting pricing discrepancies helps authorities identify recurring problems and ensures businesses remain accountable. Consumer awareness and active enforcement go hand in hand in creating a fair marketplace. The issue of missing price tags may appear small compared to larger economic challenges facing the nation, but it speaks to a much bigger principle of fairness.

At a time when households are making difficult choices about budgets, food, school expenses, transport, and daily necessities, every cent counts.

Shoppers deserve certainty, honesty, and respect when they enter a supermarket.

Businesses that value their customers must recognise that transparency is a duty.

Clear prices build confidence, strengthens consumer trust, and creates a marketplace where both businesses and shoppers thrive fairly.

Happy shopping everyone and call out for missing price tags !

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Shopping at RB Patel is a tradition for Arieta
item.description: FOR 29-year-old Arieta Vunita, grocery shopping is much more than filling a trolley it is about caring for her family, managing her household budget and making thoughtful choices. Arieta, a mother of two with another baby on the way, has been a loyal shopper at RB Patel Supermarket for more than a decade.
item.content:encoded:
FOR 29-year-old Arieta Vunita, grocery shopping is much more than filling a trolley it is about caring for her family, managing her household budget and making thoughtful choices.

Arieta, a mother of two with another baby on the way, has been a loyal shopper at RB Patel Supermarket for more than a decade.

Living in the highlands of Naitasiri with her husband and children, she makes regular trips to either the Suva or Nausori branches to stock up on everything her family needs.

For her, shopping at RB Patel Supermarket is a tradition that has been passed down through generations.

“I have been shopping at RB Patel for the past 10 years,” she says.

“My parents used to shop here when I was growing up, and now that I have my own family, I continue that tradition because they have the best deals that are afforadable for me.

Shopping twice a week, Arieta carefully plans her purchases and spends approximately $350 on groceries. She believes that creating a shopping list and sticking to a budget is one of the most important ways families can manage rising living costs.

“My husband is the only one working, so it is really important for me to take my time when shopping and to choose the best products for my family. We all know times are getting harder. Fuel prices, global events, and the rising cost of living affect all of us. We have to use our money wisely.

One of the things Arieta appreciates most about RB Patel Supermarket is the variety available under one roof. Whether she is shopping for fresh produce, household essentials, or quality meats, she knows she can find everything she needs.

“I love shopping at RB Patel because of the amazing specials every week. The butcher department is one of my favourite sections, and the supermarket is always clean and well organised.”

She especially enjoys shopping at the Nausori branch because it is convenient when travelling from Naitasiri. However, whenever she visits Suva, she stops by the Suva branch as well.

“I love shopping at both stores because the service is always consistent. The customer service is great no matter which branch I visit.”

As an expecting mother, Arieta says budgeting has become even more important.

“With another baby on the way, it is important to manage our money carefully. Life is becoming more expensive every day, and we need to learn how to save and prepare for the future.”

When it comes to feeding her family, Arieta focuses on buying nutritious foods that keeps her children healthy and strong. “It is important to give your children healthy fruits and vegetables. Protein is also important, so we always make sure to buy good quality meat for our family.”

Saturdays is usually dedicated to meat shopping, a task her husband enjoys taking part in.

“That is when my husband comes with me and chooses the meats he wants for the family and for the children,” she says with a smile.

“We work together to make sure everyone has healthy meals throughout the week.”

Living in a rural area also means careful planning is essential. Arieta says buying in bulk helps her family save money and avoid paying higher prices at smaller village shops.

“When you live in the village, you have to prepare ahead. Buying from small shops is expensive, so I always make a list before leaving home. That way I know exactly what I need and can shop wisely.”

During every visit, Arieta notices how busy the supermarket is and believes it reflects the trust customers have in the brand.

“The supermarket is always full of shoppers,” she says. “That shows people trust the products, the prices, and the variety available in-store.”

For Arieta, RB Patel Supermarket continues to be her first choice because it helps her balance quality, affordability, and convenience while caring for the people who matter most.

“We know what is best for our family, what products to buy, and how to budget properly.

That is why I continue to choose RB Patel for all my grocery needs.”

Her message to other families is simple.

“Come and shop at RB Patel Supermarket. They have a wide range of products to choose from, great customer service, and fantastic deals every week. You won’t be disappointed.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Prioritising needs first
item.description: FOR 29-year-old Arieta Vunita, grocery shopping is an important part of providing for her family and managing household expenses wisely. Living in the highlands of Naitasiri with her husband and two children, and expecting another baby, she makes regular trips to Suva and Nausori for essential household items.
item.content:encoded:
FOR 29-year-old Arieta Vunita, grocery shopping is an important part of providing for her family and managing household expenses wisely.

Living in the highlands of Naitasiri with her husband and two children, and expecting another baby, she makes regular trips to Suva and Nausori for essential household items.

Arieta has been a dedicated customer of RB Patel Supermarket for many years, choosing the store because of its competitive prices and wide range of products.

“My parents used to shop here, and now I continue the same tradition with my own family,” she said.

As a mother managing a household on a single income, Arieta carefully plans her shopping trips. She usually shops twice a week and follows a strict budget to ensure her family gets the best value for money.

“With the cost of living increasing, it is important to spend wisely and only buy what the family needs,” she explained.

Arieta believes that preparing a shopping list before visiting the supermarket helps her avoid unnecessary spending while ensuring her family has everything required for the week.

She also appreciates being able to find a variety of products in one location, making shopping more convenient and saving both time and travel costs.

For Arieta, smart shopping is not just about saving money it is about providing the best possible life for her growing family.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: MOTORING I 30 years of serving the public
item.description: FOR more than three decades, Joseva Vakalutulawa has spent his days behind the wheel, transporting passengers across Fiji and witnessing firsthand the changes that have shaped the country’s public transport industry. Originally from Burerua, Sawakasa the veteran minibus driver has dedicated over 30 years to serving commuters, becoming one of the many unsung heroes who keep Fiji moving every day.
item.content:encoded:
FOR more than three decades, Joseva Vakalutulawa has spent his days behind the wheel, transporting passengers across Fiji and witnessing firsthand the changes that have shaped the country’s public transport industry.

Originally from Burerua, Sawakasa the veteran minibus driver has dedicated over 30 years to serving commuters, becoming one of the many unsung heroes who keep Fiji moving every day.

Reflecting on the early days of the industry, Mr Vakalutulawa recalls a time before LM licence plates were introduced by the Land Transport Authority.

He said the minibus industry began humbly in the heart of Suva, particularly around Robertson Rd where drivers operated with limited resources but a strong commitment to serving the public.

“The same vans that travelled to Lautoka were often providing transport services within the Suva-Nausori corridor,” he said.

Today, however, the industry faces mounting challenges, with rising fuel prices placing significant pressure on drivers and operators alike.

According to Mr Vakalutulawa, fuel costs have increased dramatically over the years.

He noted that 20 litres of fuel, which now costs approximately $91, could once be bought for around $40.

The sharp increase has made it increasingly difficult for public service vehicle (PSV) operators to maintain profitability while continuing to provide affordable transport services.

“The arithmetic simply doesn’t add up anymore.”

The veteran driver explained that while minibus operators would welcome a fare increase of up to 15 per cent, they remain committed to serving the public and must await approval from the Fiji Competition and Consumer Commission (FCCC).

Even if approved, any fare adjustment would reportedly only apply after 9pm.

For many operators, the combination of rising operational costs and stagnant income has created uncertainty about the future sustainability of the industry.

Despite these challenges, Mr Vakalutulawa said drivers continue to prioritise the needs of ordinary Fijians.

“When we are driving, we always have something on our minds. We are here to serve the ordinary people of Fiji, especially those who cannot afford to own a vehicle.”

He acknowledged that many families across the country are experiencing financial hardship and encouraged the public to remain patient and resilient during these difficult times.

“We are not the only country facing a fuel crisis. People need to take things slowly and support one another.”

Mr Vakalutulawa also issued a message to the Government, urging leaders to remain connected to the realities faced by working class Fijians. He called on elected representatives to engage directly with transport workers and other frontline service providers who interact daily with the public.

“We put them there, and we want them to walk the talk, many people feel that politicians make promises, but they do not always come down and listen to the struggles of the people.”

He added that while many ordinary citizens may not pay income tax directly, they contribute to government revenue through Value-Added Taxes (VAT) and other charges incurred through everyday purchases.

After more than 30 years on the road, Mr Vakalutulawa remains committed to serving the travelling public. His story highlights the growing pressures faced by Fiji’s transport sector and the urgent need for solutions that balance affordability for passengers with sustainability for operators.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Keeping your kitchen clean matters more than you think
item.description: THERE’S something undeniably comforting about walking into a sparkling-clean kitchen. The countertops are clutter-free, the sink is empty, and the entire space feels fresh and inviting.
item.content:encoded:
THERE’S something undeniably comforting about walking into a sparkling-clean kitchen.

The countertops are clutter-free, the sink is empty, and the entire space feels fresh and inviting.

While a clean kitchen certainly looks great, its benefits go far beyond appearance. Maintaining a clean kitchen is one of the smartest ways to protect your family’s health, reduce daily stress, and preserve the heart of your home.

At Sandia Green Clean, we know how much life happens in the kitchen.

From preparing meals and sharing family conversations to helping with homework at the table, the kitchen is often the busiest room in the house.

That’s why it deserves a little extra care and attention.

Here are five important reasons why keeping your kitchen clean should be a top priority.

1. A clean kitchen helps keep your family healthy:

Your kitchen may appear spotless, but harmful germs can hide in places you might not expect. Countertops, cutting boards, sink handles, and appliance surfaces can all harbor bacteria that can contaminate food and lead to illness.

In fact, studies have shown that kitchens often contain more bacteria than bathrooms due to the frequent handling of food. Regular deep cleaning helps eliminate dangerous bacteria such as E. coli and Salmonella, reducing the risk of foodborne illnesses.

Professional cleaners know where germs tend to hide and use effective cleaning methods to sanitize high-touch and hard-to-reach areas. With a professionally cleaned kitchen, you can prepare meals with greater confidence and peace of mind.

Even the smallest crumbs or spills can attract unwanted pests. Food residue under appliances, sticky spots on countertops, and overflowing trash cans can quickly become an invitation for ants, flies, cockroaches, and even rodents.

Routine kitchen cleaning removes the food sources and odors that pests seek out. Deep cleaning is especially important because it targets hidden areas that are often overlooked during everyday tidying.

At Sandia Green Clean, our eco-friendly cleaning approach helps create a sanitary environment that discourages pests naturally, keeping your kitchen fresh, clean, and free from unwanted visitors.

3. A tidy kitchen saves time and reduces stress:

Cooking in a cluttered or dirty kitchen can be frustrating. Searching for clean utensils, clearing counter space, and cleaning up old messes while trying to prepare a meal can make even simple tasks feel overwhelming.

A clean and organized kitchen makes meal preparation easier, faster, and more enjoyable. When everything is in its place, you can focus on cooking instead of cleaning.

Professional cleaning services help maintain that sense of order, allowing you to enjoy a more functional kitchen without adding another chore to your busy schedule.

4. It protects your appliances and surfaces:

Kitchen surfaces and appliances represent a significant investment in your home. Over time, grease buildup, food splatters, and acidic spills can damage countertops, stain surfaces, and reduce the lifespan of appliances.

Regular professional cleaning helps prevent long-term wear and tear by removing harmful residue before it causes damage. Using the right products is equally important.

Maintaining a clean kitchen impacts your physical health, mental clarity, and financial well-being.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Camari is a loyal customer
item.description: FOR nearly 30-years, 60-year-old Camari Serukula Rawatimo of Nakorosule, Naitasiri has been a loyal customer of RB Patel Supermarket, making frequent trips to Nausori and Suva to provide for her family’s household needs. Living in the highlands of Naitasiri, Mrs Rawatimo says shopping has become a skill she has mastered over the years.
item.content:encoded:
FOR nearly 30-years, 60-year-old Camari Serukula Rawatimo of Nakorosule, Naitasiri has been a loyal customer of RB Patel Supermarket, making frequent trips to Nausori and Suva to provide for her family’s household needs.

Living in the highlands of Naitasiri, Mrs Rawatimo says shopping has become a skill she has mastered over the years.

With experience comes wisdom, especially when it comes to budgeting, choosing quality products, and ensuring the family receives the best value for money.

“Shopping at this age is actually an advantage because we know what is best for our family,” she shared.

Although she mostly shops at the Nausori branch due to convenience, she also visits RB Patel Supermarket whenever she travels to the capital.

She says the excellent customer service, affordable prices, and wide variety of products continue to make RB Patel her trusted supermarket of choice.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
